subsystem: Linux kernel SCTP (Stream Control Transmission Protocol) chunk-builder. sctp_make_strreset_req() constructs an SCTP RE-CONFIG chunk (RFC 6525) carrying an Outgoing SSN Reset Request Parameter (SCTP_PARAM_RESET_OUT_REQUEST) and/or an Incoming SSN Reset Request Parameter (SCTP_PARAM_RESET_IN_REQUEST), each optionally followed by a caller-supplied list of 16-bit stream numbers. It is invoked when a local socket asks the stack to reset the sequence numbers of one or more SCTP streams.
provenance: Identified by the kernel-internal SCTP naming and types: the function name sctp_make_strreset_req and helpers sctp_make_reconf() / sctp_addto_chunk() / SCTP_PAD4(); the parameter enum names SCTP_PARAM_RESET_OUT_REQUEST and SCTP_PARAM_RESET_IN_REQUEST; the on-wire structs sctp_strreset_outreq / sctp_strreset_inreq with their param_hdr, request_seq, response_seq and send_reset_at_tsn fields; the kernel fixed-width types __u16 / __be16 and the byte-swap helpers htons()/htonl(); and struct sctp_association fields strreset_outseq, strreset_inseq, next_tsn. This is net/sctp/sm_make_chunk.c. The banner comment 'primevul_c_test_0211_fixed' is untrusted metadata and was ignored for the security judgement.
trust_boundary: stream_num and stream_list are attacker-influenced. They originate at the socket API: a user calls setsockopt(SCTP_RESET_STREAMS), the kernel copies the user's struct sctp_reset_streams (which contains srs_number_streams and a variable-length srs_stream_list[]) in from userspace, and that count and array are threaded down to this builder as stream_num (a 16-bit count) and stream_list (a pointer to stream_num __be16 entries). stream_num is therefore an untrusted 16-bit value that can be as large as 65535, and stream_list is an untrusted buffer whose true byte length is 2*stream_num. asoc and its sequence-number fields are internal association state and are trusted. The chunk being built is later transmitted, so the wire-format length fields also cross a trust boundary to the remote peer.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point that returns a newly allocated struct sctp_chunk* (NULL on failure). Parameters: asoc is the (trusted) SCTP association the chunk belongs to; stream_num is a 16-bit count of streams to reset; stream_list points to an array of stream_num big-endian 16-bit stream identifiers to embed in the request; out and in are booleans selecting whether an outgoing-reset parameter, an incoming-reset parameter, or both are appended. stream_num and stream_list carry data derived from a userspace setsockopt buffer, so their magnitude and contents are attacker-influenced.

stream_len is meant to be the byte size of the trailing stream list: stream_num entries times 2 bytes each. Note the destination type is __u16 while the right-hand side stream_num * sizeof(__u16) is evaluated in size_t (sizeof yields size_t / unsigned long), so the product is computed at full 64-bit width and then narrowed on assignment. outreq and inreq are stack copies of the two on-wire parameter headers that will be filled in and appended. retval holds the chunk being built. outlen and inlen are 16-bit accumulators for the total byte length of the outgoing and incoming parameters respectively.

what_the_code_does.code:
outlen = (sizeof(outreq) + stream_len) * out;
	inlen = (sizeof(inreq) + stream_len) * in;
what_the_code_does.explanation: Each parameter's total length is the fixed header size (sizeof(outreq) / sizeof(inreq), the on-wire struct including param_hdr and the sequence fields) plus stream_len bytes of stream list. The multiply by the boolean out / in is a branchless conditional: when out is false the whole expression is 0, meaning 'no outgoing parameter.' As on line 7, the additions and the multiply are performed in size_t but the result is stored back into 16-bit outlen / inlen, so any value above 65535 is truncated modulo 65536.

sctp_make_reconf() allocates the RE-CONFIG chunk with room for SCTP_PAD4(outlen) + SCTP_PAD4(inlen) bytes of parameters, where SCTP_PAD4 rounds each length up to a 4-byte boundary as SCTP requires. This allocation size is derived from the possibly-truncated 16-bit outlen / inlen values, NOT from stream_len. The NULL check on lines 17-18 correctly aborts on allocation failure, so there is no unchecked-NULL use of retval below.

If an outgoing parameter was requested (outlen != 0) the stack fills the parameter header: the type constant, the on-wire length as htons(outlen) (big-endian), and three association-derived sequence numbers (outgoing reset sequence, one-less-than the incoming reset sequence, and one-less-than next_tsn) converted with htonl. sctp_addto_chunk() then appends the fixed sizeof(outreq) header bytes to the chunk's data area (skb tail). The advertised on-wire length outreq.param_hdr.length is set from outlen, the value that also drove the allocation.

After the header, if there is a stream list (stream_len != 0) the code appends stream_len bytes copied from the untrusted stream_list buffer into the chunk. Critically, the copy length here is stream_len, whereas the space reserved for this parameter was SCTP_PAD4(outlen) with outlen = sizeof(outreq) + stream_len computed and truncated independently on line 13. The safety of this append depends entirely on outlen having faithfully accounted for stream_len.

Symmetric handling for the incoming-reset parameter: fill the shorter inreq header (type, htons(inlen), and request_seq = outgoing reset seq plus the boolean out so the two requests get distinct sequence numbers), append sizeof(inreq) header bytes, then append the same stream_len bytes of stream_list again. As on line 30 the payload copy length is stream_len while the reservation was SCTP_PAD4(inlen); the two must agree for the append to stay in bounds.

Returns the assembled chunk to the caller for queuing and transmission. By this point the chunk's data area has had, potentially, an outgoing header + stream list and an incoming header + stream list appended into the buffer that sctp_make_reconf() sized from outlen/inlen.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Incorrect type conversion / numeric truncation (CWE-704 / CWE-197). stream_num is a 16-bit count fully controlled by userspace and may be up to 65535. stream_num * sizeof(__u16) is evaluated in size_t and can reach 131070, but the result is stored into the 16-bit stream_len, so it is reduced modulo 65536. For any stream_num >= 32768 the stored stream_len no longer equals the true byte length (2*stream_num) of the stream_list buffer: e.g. stream_num = 32768 yields stream_len = 0, and stream_num = 32769 yields stream_len = 2. This desynchronizes the length variable that is used BOTH to size the parameter and to bound the payload copy from the actual size of the attacker-supplied buffer, and is the root incorrect-conversion that feeds the overflow below. Invariant: the byte length used for allocation and for copying must equal 2*stream_num computed in a width that cannot overflow (do the arithmetic in size_t/int and bound stream_num before narrowing).

what_could_go_wrong.code:
outlen = (sizeof(outreq) + stream_len) * out;
	inlen = (sizeof(inreq) + stream_len) * in;
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Second truncation that turns the desync into an allocation-vs-copy size mismatch (CWE-190 integer overflow leading to CWE-787 out-of-bounds write). Even when stream_len is a large legitimate value (say 65534, from stream_num = 32767), sizeof(outreq) + stream_len = 16 + 65534 = 65550 is truncated to 16-bit outlen = 14. sctp_make_reconf() on line 16 then reserves only SCTP_PAD4(14) = 16 bytes for the outgoing parameter, yet the guard 'if (outlen)' on line 20 is true (14 != 0), so the code proceeds to append sizeof(outreq) header bytes AND, on line 30, stream_len = 65534 payload bytes. The reservation (16 bytes) is far smaller than what is written (~16 + 65534 bytes), producing a heap/skb-tail out-of-bounds write of tens of kilobytes of attacker-controlled data — kernel memory corruption. Invariant: the length used to size the chunk (outlen/inlen) must be computed at a width wide enough to hold sizeof(header) + 2*stream_num and must not be able to wrap below the number of bytes actually appended.

The payload copy is bounded by stream_len, a value derived on a DIFFERENT truncation path than the outlen that sized the allocation. Because the guard here is 'if (stream_len)' rather than a check that the remaining reserved space in retval can hold stream_len bytes, sctp_addto_chunk() will happily advance the skb tail past the reserved end when outlen underestimated the total (the scenario above). The same defect is duplicated at lines 40-41 for the incoming parameter, where stream_list is appended a second time under the same unchecked stream_len. Invariant: every sctp_addto_chunk() append must be bounded by the space actually reserved in the chunk; the builder must reject or clamp when stream_num is large enough that the 16-bit length fields cannot represent the true size.

Even absent memory corruption, the on-wire parameter length is set from the truncated outlen (and inlen on line 35). If truncation occurs, the transmitted SCTP RE-CONFIG parameter advertises a length that is inconsistent with the number of stream bytes actually placed after it, yielding malformed chunks that can confuse or crash a remote peer's parser (protocol-level CWE-20 / malformed length). This is a lower-severity consequence of the same 16-bit length model but worth confirming because it changes what the receiver reads.

These dereference asoc. If a caller ever invoked this builder with asoc == NULL it would be a NULL pointer dereference (CWE-476). In the SCTP call graph asoc is always a live, non-NULL association, so this is trusted and low risk; it is called out only for completeness. The subtractions strreset_inseq - 1 and next_tsn - 1 are on trusted internal counters and are not attacker-controlled.

summary: This SCTP RE-CONFIG stream-reset chunk builder computes all of its length quantities (stream_len, outlen, inlen) in 16-bit __u16 variables even though the underlying arithmetic (stream_num * 2, plus a fixed header) is done in size_t and can exceed 65535 for large userspace-supplied stream_num. The resulting truncation (CWE-704/CWE-197) lets the length that SIZES the chunk (outlen/inlen) diverge from the length that BOUNDS the payload copy (stream_len), so a crafted stream count can make sctp_make_reconf() under-allocate while sctp_addto_chunk() copies the full, larger stream list — a kernel heap/skb out-of-bounds write (CWE-787). Safety hinges entirely on the caller strictly bounding stream_num before this point.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: sctp_make_strreset_req(asoc, stream_num, stream_list, out, in) signature (lines 2-5)
reviewed_anchors.reason: stream_num (16-bit count) and stream_list (untrusted buffer) are the attacker-influenced inputs whose relationship to the 16-bit length fields is unsound
size_t product truncated into 16-bit stream_len — incorrect type conversion / truncation, the root desync
16-bit truncation of the allocation length; wraps below the bytes actually appended
same 16-bit truncation for the incoming parameter length
allocation is sized from the truncated outlen/inlen rather than from stream_len, creating the size mismatch
correct allocation-failure guard; retval is not used unchecked
gates on the truncated length, so a truncated-nonzero outlen still enters the append path with a large stream_len
advertises the truncated length on the wire, producing malformed parameters
fixed-size header appends of sizeof(struct); constant length, not attacker-scaled
payload copy bounded by stream_len rather than by remaining reserved space; the write site of the overflow
trusted internal association counters; asoc is non-NULL in the SCTP call graph
bool is 0/1; intended select-or-zero idiom, not itself a vulnerability
each padded value is <= ~65536 and the sum is computed in int; the padding itself does not overflow — the danger is upstream truncation of outlen/inlen

findings.title: 16-bit truncation of parameter length under-allocates chunk while full stream list is copied (heap OOB write)
findings.anchor: outlen = (sizeof(outreq) + stream_len) * out; ... sctp_make_reconf(...) ; sctp_addto_chunk(retval, stream_len, stream_list)
findings.bug_class: Integer truncation causing allocation/copy size mismatch -> heap out-of-bounds write
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0097_cwe704.c (and the appended-to buffer in sctp_make_reconf/sctp_addto_chunk, implementation file not provided)
findings.invariant: The length used to reserve space in the chunk must be computed at a width that cannot overflow (>= sizeof(header) + 2*stream_num) and must be >= the number of bytes subsequently appended for that parameter; equivalently, stream_num must be bounded so sizeof(outreq)+2*stream_num fits in the 16-bit length field before it is used.
findings.failure_mode: With attacker-influenced stream_num chosen so sizeof(outreq) + 2*stream_num exceeds 65535 (e.g. stream_num = 32767 -> stream_len = 65534, outlen = (16+65534) & 0xffff = 14), sctp_make_reconf reserves only SCTP_PAD4(14)=16 bytes, but 'if (outlen)' is still true and sctp_addto_chunk then appends sizeof(outreq) header bytes plus stream_len=65534 payload bytes, writing ~65550 bytes into a 16-byte reservation. Result is a kernel skb-tail/heap out-of-bounds write of attacker-controlled data, i.e. remote-adjacent memory corruption and likely privilege escalation or panic. The incoming-reset branch (inlen, lines 33-41) has the identical defect.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether every caller path into sctp_make_strreset_req bounds stream_num so that sizeof(struct sctp_strreset_outreq) + stream_num*2 <= 65535, and whether sctp_addto_chunk/sctp_make_reconf perform any independent tail-room check; if not, this is an exploitable OOB write.

findings.title: Incorrect type conversion: stream list byte length computed in size_t then truncated to __u16
findings.bug_class: Incorrect type conversion / numeric truncation of an untrusted length
findings.invariant: The byte length of the stream list must faithfully represent 2*stream_num; the multiplication must be carried in a width (size_t or int) that holds up to 131070 and stream_num must be bounded before the value is narrowed to 16 bits.
findings.failure_mode: sizeof(__u16) is size_t, so stream_num * sizeof(__u16) is evaluated in 64-bit but assigned to the 16-bit stream_len, discarding the high bit for stream_num >= 32768. The stored length then diverges from the actual 2*stream_num size of stream_list: it can wrap to 0 (dropping the copy entirely, a logic/interop bug) or, combined with the outlen truncation on lines 13-14, drive the allocation/copy mismatch that overflows the chunk. This is the root incorrect-conversion feeding finding #1.
findings.what_to_confirm: That stream_num is validated (e.g. against asoc->stream.outcnt/incnt) to a value small enough that 2*stream_num cannot wrap the 16-bit stream_len, and that no path reaches here with stream_num >= 32768.

findings.title: On-wire parameter length advertised from truncated value
findings.bug_class: Malformed protocol length field from truncated internal length
findings.invariant: The RE-CONFIG parameter length placed on the wire must equal the number of bytes actually serialized for that parameter (header + stream list).
findings.failure_mode: When outlen/inlen truncate, the transmitted parameter header advertises a length inconsistent with the bytes that follow, producing malformed SCTP chunks that can confuse or crash a peer's RE-CONFIG parser. This is a downstream symptom of the same 16-bit length model rather than an independent memory bug, hence low severity.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether a receiving SCTP implementation tolerates or rejects a stream-reset parameter whose param_hdr.length does not match the trailing stream-list byte count.

audit_checklist: Trace every caller of sctp_make_strreset_req (SCTP_RESET_STREAMS setsockopt path -> sctp_send_reset_streams -> sctp_stream_reset) and confirm stream_num is clamped so sizeof(struct sctp_strreset_outreq) + 2*stream_num <= 65535 before this function runs.
Widen stream_len, outlen and inlen to size_t/u32 (or bound stream_num) so the length computations on lines 7, 13, 14 cannot truncate.
Verify that sctp_make_reconf reserves space based on the same length that bounds every sctp_addto_chunk append, and that sctp_addto_chunk asserts remaining tailroom >= the copy length.
Confirm the guards on lines 20/33 (if outlen / if inlen) and 29/40 (if stream_len) cannot be true while the reservation is smaller than the bytes appended.
Check that the on-wire param_hdr.length (lines 22, 35) always equals the serialized parameter size, and that a peer validates it.
Confirm asoc is guaranteed non-NULL at every call site for the dereferences on lines 23-25 and 36.
